description: This figure presents engineering stress-strain curves of a fully austenitic 0.92C-28.8Mn-9.4Al (wt.%) low-density steel for automotive applications, with 6 samples of different austenite grain sizes (1.65 μm, 2.2 μm, 8.4 μm, 12.4 μm, 21 μm, 40 μm) tested. The x-axis represents elongation in %, and the y-axis represents engineering stress in MPa. It is observed that smaller grain size leads to higher yield strength and ultimate tensile strength, but lower elongation: the 1.65 μm grain sample reaches a tensile strength close to 1000 MPa with ~47% total elongation, while the 40 μm grain sample shows ~800 MPa tensile strength and ~61% total elongation. Elongation shows a saturation effect when grain size exceeds 10 μm. The results validate that the mechanical properties of this alloy follow the Hall-Petch relation, demonstrating that grain size adjustment is an effective approach to tune the strength-formability balance of this low-density steel for automotive lightweighting.
